items.title: Training an image classifier on millions of photos
items.scenario: A team trains a ResNet on 1.2M ImageNet images. Computing the gradient on all images before each update would require a forward and backward pass over the full set—prohibitively slow. They use mini-batch SGD with batch size 256: each GPU step processes 256 random images, updates weights, and completes an epoch in roughly 1.2M/256 ≈ 4,700 steps.
items.explanation: Mini-batch SGD trades exact full-batch direction for thousands of cheap, noisy updates per epoch, which is how large-scale vision models are trained in practice.

items.title: Language model pretraining
items.scenario: A 7B-parameter language model sees billions of tokens. The data loader streams token sequences into batches of shape (batch_size, seq_len). Each step computes loss on that batch only; gradients are averaged across the batch before the optimizer step.
items.explanation: SGD-style batched updates let training proceed incrementally on data that never fits in memory at once—batching is both an optimization choice and a memory constraint.

items.title: Online fraud detection with streaming data
items.scenario: Fraud labels arrive continuously. The system maintains model weights and applies SGD with batch size 1 (or small micro-batches) on each new labeled transaction, updating within milliseconds.
items.explanation: Pure or small-batch SGD fits streaming settings where waiting for a full dataset pass is impossible; each example triggers an immediate approximate gradient step.
